h1Title: Burn Scar Contracture: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Burn scar contracture occurs when scar tissue formed after a thermal, chemical, or electrical burn shortens and tightens across a joint or body surface, restricting movement and causing functional impairment. Contractures affect an estimated 30-50% of patients admitted to burn units and are the leading cause of long-term disability following burns. They develop most aggressively over joints such as the neck, axilla, elbow, hand, and knee, and can appear within weeks of wound closure. Without early rehabilitation, pressure therapy, and splinting, even moderate burns can produce contractures severe enough to prevent extension of a limb. Treatment ranges from scar massage and splinting for early contractures to Z-plasty, skin grafting, and flap reconstruction for severe deformities.

keyStats.prevalence: Contractures develop in approximately 30-50% of patients hospitalized for burns (Leblebici et al. 2006; Serghiou et al. 2009)
keyStats.demographics: Children under 5 years and adults over 60 have the highest risk; hand and neck contractures are most common overall
keyStats.avgAge: No age restriction; pediatric burns disproportionately produce growth-restricting contractures as the child develops
keyStats.globalCases: Roughly 11 million burns requiring medical attention annually (WHO 2024); contracture is the most common long-term complication

definition: Burn scar contracture (ICD-10: L90.5) is the progressive shortening and thickening of immature scar tissue that bridges a joint or spans a functionally mobile area, resulting in restricted range of motion, postural deformity, and, in severe cases, joint dislocation. The pathophysiology involves excessive myofibroblast activity in the healing dermis: myofibroblasts synthesize collagen in a disorganized, parallel-bundle pattern and generate mechanical contraction forces that draw wound edges together. This process is adaptive for wound closure but becomes pathologic when it persists beyond 3-4 weeks, particularly over joints where skin excursion during movement is greatest. Contractures are classified by depth (superficial dermal, deep dermal, full-thickness), by anatomical location (neck, axilla, antecubital, palmar, popliteal, perioral), and by severity — linear or band contractures limit extension in a single plane, while sheet contractures immobilize a broad skin region. Hypertrophic scars (raised, erythematous, confined to the wound bed) frequently accompany contractures but are distinct entities; keloids overgrow the original wound margin and are primarily cosmetic. Plastic and reconstructive surgeons manage burn contracture in collaboration with occupational therapists, physiotherapists, and orthotists.

typesClassification.description: A discrete fibrous band crossing a joint line that limits extension in one plane. Common over the antecubital fossa, axilla, and neck. Amenable to Z-plasty, W-plasty, or local flap release with high success rates.

Broad, confluent scar involving the entire surface of a joint and the surrounding skin. Requires wide excision and resurfacing with skin graft or flap. Functional improvement is significant but rarely complete.

Scar bridges the palm or individual finger joints, causing flexion deformity. Intrinsic hand function is often preserved if skeletal injury is absent. Staged release with full-thickness grafts gives best functional results.

Scar band tethers chin to sternum, limiting neck extension and, in severe cases, displacing the lower lip (ectropion of lip). Functional interference with airway management, eating, and vision is possible.
Burn scar bridges axilla and medial upper arm, restricting shoulder abduction. Classified by Huang grade I-IV; grade III-IV requires flap reconstruction to restore the axillary hollow.

primarySymptoms: Progressive tightness and pulling sensation over a burned joint that worsens during movement, often beginning 2-4 weeks after wound closure as immature scar matures.
Reduced range of motion at the affected joint — measured by goniometry; extension loss across the elbow, knee, or neck is the commonest finding.
Visible fibrous band or thickened rope of scar tissue crossing a joint crease, blanching on stretch and releasing with joint flexion.
Redness, warmth, and firmness of immature hypertrophic scar overlying the contracture during the active proliferative phase (typically months 1-18 post-burn).
Pruritus (itching) of the contracting scar, rated moderate-to-severe by more than 60% of burn survivors in the first year.
Pain on stretching or active use of the affected joint, particularly after periods of immobility such as overnight sleep.
Postural compensation and biomechanical alteration — patients adopt protective postures (shoulder elevation, elbow flexion, neck flexion) that compound deformity over time.
Skin breakdown and ulceration at the apex of a tight contracture band where skin thins over repeated stretch cycles.
In children, growth restriction and progressive deformity as the skeleton outgrows the inelastic scar overlay, causing secondary joint deformity.
earlyWarningSigns: Loss of full passive extension at any burn-adjacent joint when measured by goniometry during inpatient rehabilitation — even 10-15 degrees of deficit early on predicts significant contracture without intervention.
Visible scar blanching during active joint use within 2-3 weeks of wound closure, indicating active myofibroblast contraction.
Failure to achieve a flat, pliable scar surface by week 6 post-healing, with persisting induration and erythema.
Child reporting avoidance of movement or activity in the burned limb, a behavioral signal of early contracture discomfort.
emergencySigns: Acute neurovascular compromise — pallor, pulselessness, paresthesia, or absent capillary refill distal to a contracting scar band — requires urgent surgical release.
Neck contracture severe enough to restrict jaw opening (trismus) or cause difficulty breathing, particularly relevant in pediatric patients or prior to planned anesthesia.
Rapidly progressive contracture with joint subluxation or dislocation visible on plain radiograph, requiring urgent surgical consultation.
Skin ulceration or full-thickness breakdown at the apex of a contracture band, with signs of deep-space infection (fever, cellulitis, purulent discharge).

causes.description: Destruction of the dermis triggers the wound-healing cascade. If the full dermis is lost, re-epithelialization from wound edges cannot restore normal pliable skin; immature scar tissue forms instead. Deeper and larger burns produce more myofibroblast-rich granulation tissue and thus more contracture.

Alkali burns (cement, household lye) cause liquefactive necrosis that penetrates deeply; acid burns cause coagulative necrosis. Both destroy dermal appendages required for normal healing, leaving fibrotic scar. Chemical burns to the hand and face carry high contracture rates.

Entry and exit wounds plus internal current-path tissue destruction leave full-thickness defects. The surrounding tissue often sustains thermal injury from electrical arc flash. Contractures develop at entry/exit sites and wherever arc flash damages the extremities.

High-dose radiation (radiotherapy, radiation accidents) causes progressive endarteritis, dermal fibrosis, and poor wound healing that can contract over years, producing late-onset contractures distinct from acute burn injuries.

The key cellular mechanism in all contractures. TGF-β1 from platelets and macrophages drives fibroblast-to-myofibroblast transition. Myofibroblasts express α-smooth muscle actin stress fibers and generate contractile force. Normally these cells undergo apoptosis once the wound closes; in pathological contracture they persist for months.

Mechanical stretching counteracts myofibroblast contraction. Patients who cannot tolerate movement (pain, sedation, concurrent illness) or who lack access to rehabilitation accumulate contracture faster because unopposed scar shortening is not reversed by daily range-of-motion exercises.

riskFactors.factor: Deep partial-thickness or full-thickness burn depth
riskFactors.description: Superficial dermal burns heal with minimal scarring; deep dermal and full-thickness burns destroy dermal appendages, requiring granulation and prolonged healing. Risk of contracture scales with burn depth: full-thickness burns produce contracture in over 70% of cases spanning a joint.

Skin over joints experiences repeated stretch with every movement. Scar crossing joint creases is under constant mechanical stress, amplifying contraction. Joint surface area relative to overall body surface area is highest in children, increasing their risk.

riskFactors.factor: Large total body surface area (TBSA) burn
riskFactors.description: Burns covering more than 20% TBSA are associated with prolonged healing, sepsis, and reduced mobility — all of which accelerate contracture. Patients requiring prolonged sedation and mechanical ventilation have the least rehabilitation input.

riskFactors.factor: Delayed wound closure (>14-21 days to healing)
riskFactors.description: Wounds closing in under 14 days have low contracture rates; those taking more than 21 days have rates above 50%. Early surgical excision and grafting within 48-72 hours of injury reduces time to closure.

Children have thinner skin and a higher ratio of joint surface area to body size. Additionally, skeletal growth outpaces inelastic scar, producing progressive contracture deformity over years even when initial rehabilitation was adequate.

Individuals with higher melanocyte density have higher risk of hypertrophic and keloidal scarring, which is closely associated with contracture formation. Hypertrophic scars are 2-3 times more common in Fitzpatrick IV-VI skin types.

riskFactors.factor: Inadequate access to burn rehabilitation services
riskFactors.description: Low- and middle-income countries bear over 90% of burn deaths and disability. Pressure therapy garments, orthotist-fabricated splints, and physiotherapy are unavailable to many patients, and contracture rates are substantially higher as a result.

affectedDemographics: Children under 5 years: highest risk of scalds from hot liquids; account for 40-50% of pediatric burn unit admissions in high-income countries; growth-driven contracture worsens throughout childhood.
Adults in low- and middle-income countries where flame burns from cooking fires and kerosene lamps are common; WHO estimates 96% of fatal burns occur in LMICs.
Occupational groups: workers in manufacturing, construction, and food service exposed to chemical, electrical, and thermal hazards.
Elderly patients (over 60): thinner skin, reduced mobility, and comorbidities impair rehabilitation adherence, increasing contracture rates compared with younger adults.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of burn scar contracture is clinical, based on burn history, inspection of the scar, and objective measurement of joint range of motion. The evaluating clinician documents burn cause, depth, anatomical location, time from injury to wound closure, and rehabilitation history. Physical examination identifies visible scar bands, skin tethering, loss of natural joint creases, and the patient's resting posture. Joint range of motion is quantified with a standard goniometer; deficit greater than 15 degrees from normal establishes clinically significant contracture. The Vancouver Scar Scale (VSS) grades scar vascularity, pliability, pigmentation, and height to guide treatment and track response. The Patient and Observer Scar Assessment Scale (POSAS) adds patient-reported outcomes on pain and itch. Plain radiographs are obtained when joint subluxation or secondary skeletal deformity is suspected. For complex reconstruction planning, CT with 3D reconstruction or MRI delineates the extent of deeper involvement. Functional assessment by an occupational therapist quantifies activities of daily living impairment and guides goal-setting. Scar assessment and range-of-motion measurement are repeated at regular intervals (typically monthly during the active scar phase) to determine whether conservative treatment is achieving adequate progress before surgical referral.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies joint mobility deficit and tracks progress over serial visits
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The therapist or clinician places a handheld goniometer over the joint axis and records active and passive motion in degrees. Painless; completed in minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Grades scar vascularity, pliability, pigmentation, and height to classify scar maturity and severity
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician visually and manually assesses the scar, assigning 0-14 total score. No equipment needed beyond inspection and light palpation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies secondary skeletal deformity, joint subluxation, dislocation, or heterotopic ossification
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard X-ray taking seconds. Two views (AP and lateral) of the joint are obtained. No preparation required.

diagnosticTests.test: Ultrasonography of scar and underlying tissue
diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures scar thickness, echogenicity, and depth to plan surgical release
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Gel applied to the scar; high-frequency probe produces a painless image in 5-10 minutes. No radiation.

diagnosticTests.test: Durometer or tonometer scar stiffness measurement
diagnosticTests.purpose: Objective assessment of scar hardness to complement clinical VSS and guide pressure therapy adjustment
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Small handheld device pressed gently on the scar for 2-3 seconds. Painless.

diagnosticTests.test: MRI of joint and scar (pre-surgical planning)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Delineates extent of deep fibrosis, identifies involvement of neurovascular structures, and guides reconstructive planning
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-45 minute scan lying still; no radiation; gadolinium contrast may be used. Results available within 24 hours.

treatmentOverview: Management of burn scar contracture follows a stepwise approach progressing from conservative to surgical intervention based on contracture severity, patient age, and functional impact. Conservative treatment begins immediately after wound closure and aims to prevent or limit contracture through three core pillars: range-of-motion exercises, splinting and positioning, and pressure therapy. Custom thermoplastic splints hold joints in the anti-contracture position (extension for most joints, finger abduction for the hand) during sleep and rest periods. Compression garments delivering 15-25 mmHg of interface pressure, worn 23 hours per day for 12-24 months, reduce hypertrophic scar bulk and slow contracture progression. Scar massage and silicone gel sheet application are adjuncts with moderate evidence for improving pliability. Intralesional corticosteroid injections (triamcinolone 10-40 mg/mL) reduce hypertrophic scar volume and may slow early band formation. Laser therapy — fractional CO₂ and pulsed-dye laser — has strong evidence for improving scar pliability and reducing contracture in early treatment, with fractional ablative laser reshaping collagen architecture in immature scars. When conservative treatment fails to achieve adequate function after 6-12 months, or when contracture is severe at presentation, surgical release is indicated. Z-plasty is the most widely used technique for linear bands, using triangular skin flaps to reorient scar tension lines and lengthen the contracting band by 25-75% depending on flap angle. Sheet contractures require excision and resurfacing with split-thickness or full-thickness skin grafts, or local, regional, or free flaps when graft take is poor or cosmetically critical areas are involved. Postoperative rehabilitation with splinting, compression, and physiotherapy is essential to maintain surgical gains.

medicalTreatments.name: Pressure garment therapy (15-25 mmHg, 23 h/day)
medicalTreatments.description: Custom-fitted compression garments worn continuously from wound closure until scar maturation, typically 12-24 months. Mechanism: pressure reduces dermal blood flow and collagen production, flattening hypertrophic scar. Compliance is the main barrier to effectiveness.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces scar height and hypertrophy by 30-50% in randomized trials; consistent use produces significantly better outcomes than intermittent use

medicalTreatments.name: Splinting and serial casting in anti-contracture position
medicalTreatments.description: Thermoplastic static splints hold joints at maximal tolerated extension during rest periods; serial casting progressively stretches established contractures by sequential cast changes every 3-7 days.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Prevents new contracture in over 80% of compliant patients; serial casting achieves 15-30 degrees additional range of motion per casting cycle in established contractures

medicalTreatments.name: Intralesional triamcinolone acetonide (10-40 mg/mL)
medicalTreatments.description: Injected directly into hypertrophic scar every 4-6 weeks. Reduces collagen synthesis, promotes scar flattening, and decreases itch. Side-effects include skin atrophy, depigmentation, and telangiectasia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces scar thickness by 40-70% in hypertrophic scars; response rate approximately 70-80% for early contracture bands

medicalTreatments.name: Fractional ablative CO₂ laser
medicalTreatments.description: Creates thousands of microscopic ablative columns in the scar, triggering collagen remodeling without full-surface ablation. Reduces scar stiffness, erythema, and contracture. Multiple sessions (3-6) at 4-8 week intervals are standard.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improves VSS score by 30-60% over 3-6 sessions in multiple cohort studies; combined with intralesional therapy shows additive benefit

medicalTreatments.name: Silicone gel sheet and topical silicone
medicalTreatments.description: Applied to mature closed wounds; hydrates the stratum corneum and reduces transepidermal water loss, believed to suppress TGF-β and fibroblast activity. Minimal side-effects, suitable for pediatric use.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Meta-analyses show 30-40% reduction in scar elevation and erythema; modest effect on established contracture but strong preventive evidence

medicalTreatments.name: Physiotherapy with sustained stretching and active range-of-motion exercises
medicalTreatments.description: Daily supervised exercises combined with heat and massage. Mechanical tension on collagen fibers promotes fiber realignment and reduces cross-linking. Essential adjunct to all other treatments.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Maintains surgical gains and prevents recurrence; patients completing supervised physiotherapy achieve 15-20% greater range of motion than controls at 12 months

surgicalOptions.name: Z-plasty contracture release
surgicalOptions.description: Triangular skin flaps transposed across a linear scar band to reorient scar tension lines and lengthen the contracting segment. Standard Z-plasty (60° flaps) increases length by 75%. Multiple Z-plasties used for long linear bands.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Excellent outcomes for linear neck, axilla, and antecubital bands; recurrence rate under 15% with postoperative rehabilitation; provides durable functional improvement in 85-90% of cases

surgicalOptions.name: Skin grafting (split-thickness or full-thickness) after contracture release
surgicalOptions.description: Wide excision of the contracted scar followed by resurfacing with autologous skin graft. Split-thickness grafts (0.012-0.018 inch) from the thigh harvest site are used for large areas; full-thickness grafts from inguinal or retroauricular donors give better cosmesis and less secondary contraction for hand and face.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Primary graft take over 90% in planned procedures; secondary contracture of split-thickness grafts occurs in 20-40% without compression; full-thickness grafts have 5-15% secondary contraction rate

surgicalOptions.name: Local and regional flap reconstruction
surgicalOptions.description: Rotational or advancement flaps from adjacent unburned tissue. Provides vascularized, pliable, color-matched coverage over critical areas (hand dorsum, face, neck). Superior to grafts where recipient bed is poor or donor tissue is unavailable.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Flap survival above 95% in experienced hands; functional outcomes superior to grafts in most joint-spanning reconstructions

surgicalOptions.name: Free flap reconstruction for complex defects
surgicalOptions.description: Microsurgical transfer of distant tissue (anterolateral thigh, radial forearm, latissimus dorsi flaps) for large defects where local tissue is insufficient. Requires microsurgery expertise and operating theatre time of 4-8 hours.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Flap success rate over 95% in burn-specialized microsurgery centres; provides durable long-term reconstruction for major contractures

alternativeTreatments.name: Botulinum toxin A injections into perilesional musculature
alternativeTreatments.description: Investigational use alongside surgical release; reduces underlying muscle tension across a joint, potentially limiting early postoperative contracture recurrence. Evidence from small cohort studies is promising but not yet guideline-level.

alternativeTreatments.name: Platelet-rich plasma (PRP) injection
alternativeTreatments.description: Autologous PRP injected into hypertrophic scar provides growth factors and cytokines that may shift the scar microenvironment toward remodeling. Pilot RCTs show improvements in VSS score; larger trials are ongoing.

whySeeSpecialist: A plastic and reconstructive surgeon should evaluate any burn contracture limiting joint range of motion by more than 15 degrees or causing functional impairment beyond 3-6 months of conservative treatment. Earlier specialist review is warranted for pediatric patients, contractures threatening neurovascular function, or neck contractures restricting airway access.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a plastic surgeon with subspecialty training in burn reconstruction who operates at a burn center or tertiary referral unit. Ask about their annual volume of contracture releases, experience with microsurgical flaps, and access to multidisciplinary occupational therapy and splinting services. Pediatric cases benefit from a center integrating pediatric anesthesia and child life specialists.
hospitalCriteria: Verified burn center designation or affiliation with a burn unit (American Burn Association-verified or equivalent)
Multidisciplinary team including plastic surgeons, occupational therapists, physiotherapists, and certified orthotists
Microsurgical capability for complex flap reconstruction
Pediatric-specific rehabilitation program if children are treated
Pressure garment fabrication service on-site or by established referral
keyFacilities: ABA-verified burn centers (US) or equivalent national burn units
Tertiary plastic and reconstructive surgery departments
preventionStrategies: Achieve wound closure within 14 days of injury through early surgical excision and grafting; wounds open beyond 21 days have contracture rates above 50%.
Apply custom thermoplastic splints in the anti-contracture position from the day of wound closure, worn nightly throughout scar maturation.
Begin range-of-motion exercises within 24-48 hours of wound closure, progressing to active stretching at every therapy session.
Fit pressure garments at wound closure and maintain 15-25 mmHg interface pressure for 12-24 months.
Apply silicone gel sheeting to healed burn surfaces from week 3-4 to slow hypertrophic scar maturation.
Schedule monthly scar assessments in the first year to detect early contracture band formation before it becomes functionally limiting.
lifestyleModifications: Wear pressure garments consistently for the prescribed hours per day (23 h/day during the active phase); gaps in wear allow scar rebound.
Perform the complete daily stretching and exercise program from occupational therapy, even when the scar is itchy or slightly uncomfortable.
Sleep in anti-contracture splints throughout the 12-24 month scar maturation period.
Keep the scar well moisturized with fragrance-free emollient twice daily to prevent skin drying and cracking that limits stretch tolerance.
Protect maturing scar from sun exposure with SPF 50 sunscreen or clothing; UV can cause post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation and delay maturation.
Maintain a healthy body weight; excess adipose tissue limits joint mobility and makes garment fitting difficult.
Attend all scheduled scar clinic appointments; range-of-motion decline of 10 degrees between visits is an actionable signal requiring treatment escalation.

recommended: Protein intake of 1.5-2 g/kg/day during the first 12-18 months to support collagen synthesis and skin graft healing
Vitamin C (75-90 mg/day or higher during healing) as an essential cofactor for collagen cross-linking
Zinc-rich foods (meat, legumes, nuts) to support wound healing and immune function
Adequate calories (1.5-1.7× basal energy expenditure) to prevent catabolism during the hypermetabolic phase post-burn
avoid: Tobacco smoking — nicotine impairs wound perfusion, reduces graft take, and doubles hypertrophic scar incidence
Excessive alcohol, which suppresses immune function and impairs wound healing
Highly inflammatory diets high in processed sugars, which may sustain TGF-β-driven fibrosis

exerciseGuidelines: Active range-of-motion exercises at the affected joint should be performed at least three times daily from wound closure. Gentle sustained stretching (30-60 seconds per joint position) is more effective than ballistic stretching. Hydrotherapy and pool exercise facilitate movement with reduced pain. Post-surgical patients resume rehabilitation within 5-7 days of grafting once graft take is confirmed by the surgical team.
dailyManagement: Perform a complete range-of-motion exercise routine morning, afternoon, and evening, holding each stretch for 30-60 seconds.
Apply and reapply pressure garment immediately after exercises or showering, checking for areas of excess pressure or garment slippage.
Inspect the scar and surrounding skin daily for redness, skin breakdown, or new tightness.
Apply prescribed silicone sheet under the pressure garment and replace every 12 hours if daily-wash schedule is followed.
Record joint range-of-motion measurements weekly with a goniometer at home if trained, bringing the log to clinic appointments.
Report any sudden increase in tightness, skin breakdown, or color change to the burn scar clinic promptly.
prognosis: Prognosis depends on burn depth, joint location, and speed of intervention. Superficial burns healing within 14 days rarely produce functionally limiting contractures. Deep burns managed with early excision, grafting, and immediate rehabilitation achieve clinically acceptable range of motion in 75-85% of affected joints at 12 months. Without rehabilitation, functional deficits persist in 50-70% of deep burns over joints. Children face the additional challenge of scar outgrowth during skeletal growth, requiring repeated surgical revisions; studies show up to 30% of pediatric patients need at least one surgical release by adulthood. Neck and hand contractures carry the greatest long-term morbidity. With modern multidisciplinary burn rehabilitation — combining surgery, physiotherapy, pressure therapy, and laser — the majority of patients achieve functional independence, although complete cosmetic restoration is rarely achievable for deep burns.
recoveryTimeline: Scar matures over 12-24 months. Range-of-motion gains from physiotherapy accumulate over the first 3-6 months. Pressure garments are typically worn for 12-24 months until scar blanches on pressure and softens to pliable. Post-surgical recovery from Z-plasty takes 2-4 weeks to resume exercises; skin graft donor and recipient sites heal in 10-14 days. Definitive functional assessment is usually made at 18-24 months post-burn.
complications: Joint subluxation or dislocation from chronic untreated contracture pulling joint surfaces out of alignment — requires orthopedic co-management and may need corrective osteotomy.

Peripheral nerve entrapment or stretch neuropathy (most often ulnar or median nerve at the elbow, or brachial plexus in severe axillary contracture) — detected by nerve conduction studies and requiring surgical decompression.
Skin breakdown and chronic ulceration at the apex of a contracture band from repetitive shear — risk of osteomyelitis in underlying bone.
Growth arrest and limb length discrepancy in children with circumferential trunk or limb burns restricting growth plate function.
Psychological impact: depression, anxiety, and post-traumatic stress disorder affect 20-40% of burn survivors; contracture-related functional limitation compounds body image distress.
Scar contracture recurrence after surgical release, occurring in 10-25% of cases without adequate postoperative rehabilitation and compression.

supportResources.name: Phoenix Society for Burn Survivors
supportResources.description: Peer support, advocacy, and rehabilitation resources for burn survivors internationally.

supportResources.name: American Burn Association Patient Resources
supportResources.description: Burn prevention, recovery guides, and ABA-verified burn center directory for the US.

supportResources.name: Changing Faces UK
supportResources.description: Psychosocial support and peer counselling for people with visible differences including burn scars.

confusedWithConditions.name: Keloid Scar
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Keloids grow beyond the original wound margin, are rarely functionally limiting, and do not cross joint lines to cause contracture. Contractures remain within the wound footprint and restrict movement. Keloids recur after excision alone; contractures require rehabilitation and splinting regardless of treatment.

confusedWithConditions.name: Neuropathic Pain Disorder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Neuropathic pain after burns can mimic contracture pain during movement but is not associated with a goniometric range-of-motion deficit. Neuropathic pain responds to gabapentinoids and amitriptyline rather than splinting and stretching.

confusedWithConditions.name: Osteoarthritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Osteoarthritis causes joint stiffness from cartilage loss and osteophyte formation, confirmed by X-ray. Burn contracture restricts motion through periarticular scar, with normal or near-normal joint space on imaging unless prolonged contracture caused secondary arthropathy.

relatedConditions.name: Keloid Scar
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are fibroproliferative disorders of wound healing managed by plastic surgery; hypertrophic scarring frequently coexists with contracture.

relatedConditions.name: Eczema
relatedConditions.relevance: Atopic dermatitis increases skin fragility and barrier dysfunction; severe eczema requiring immunosuppression can complicate burn healing and graft take.

relatedConditions.name: Neuropathic Pain
relatedConditions.relevance: Nerve injury from burns produces neuropathic pain that accompanies contracture, requiring parallel pharmacological management.

relatedConditions.name: Psoriasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Both psoriasis and burn scars are managed by plastic surgery and dermatology; psoriasis-related Koebner phenomenon can complicate healing in burn-scarred skin.

faqs.question: What is a burn scar contracture?
faqs.answer: A burn scar contracture is tightening and shortening of scar tissue across a joint after a burn injury, restricting movement. It develops as myofibroblasts in the healing wound generate contractile force. Common sites are the neck, armpit, elbow, hand, and knee. Treatment ranges from splinting and physiotherapy to surgical release.

faqs.question: How long after a burn does contracture develop?
faqs.answer: Contracture typically begins within 2-4 weeks of wound closure when immature scar starts to shorten. Active contraction continues throughout the scar maturation phase, which lasts 12-24 months. Early rehabilitation initiated within 48 hours of wound closure is the most effective way to prevent it.

faqs.question: Can burn contracture be prevented?
faqs.answer: Yes, with early intervention. Closing wounds within 14 days, beginning range-of-motion exercises within 48 hours of closure, fitting anti-contracture splints, and wearing pressure garments 23 hours per day all significantly reduce contracture rates. Access to a burn rehabilitation team immediately after wound closure is the key preventive factor.

faqs.question: What surgery is done for burn contracture?
faqs.answer: Z-plasty is the most common procedure for linear band contractures, using triangular flaps to lengthen the scar by up to 75%. Wide sheet contractures require excision and skin grafting or flap reconstruction. Surgery is followed by intensive physiotherapy, splinting, and pressure therapy to maintain the range of motion gained.

faqs.question: Does contracture surgery hurt?
faqs.answer: Surgery is performed under general or regional anesthesia, so the procedure itself is painless. Post-operative discomfort at the donor and recipient sites is managed with oral analgesics for 1-2 weeks. Rehabilitation exercises after surgery can cause stretching discomfort, which is expected and managed with physiotherapy guidance.

faqs.question: How long do you wear a pressure garment for burn contracture?
faqs.answer: Pressure garments should be worn 23 hours per day for 12-24 months until the scar matures — defined as blanching on pressure, becoming pliable, and fading from red to pale. Wearing them for fewer hours than prescribed allows scar rebound and reduces their effectiveness.

faqs.question: Is laser treatment effective for burn scar contracture?
faqs.answer: Fractional ablative CO2 laser and pulsed-dye laser improve scar pliability and reduce contracture in early and established scars. Multiple sessions (3-6) at 4-8 week intervals are needed. Studies show VSS score improvements of 30-60%. Laser works best alongside stretching, pressure, and silicone therapy rather than as a standalone treatment.

faqs.question: Can children outgrow a burn contracture?
faqs.answer: Children do not outgrow contracture — the opposite occurs. As the skeleton grows, inelastic scar tissue restricts expansion, worsening contracture and causing joint deformity over time. Pediatric patients need regular surgical revision as they grow, and around 30% require at least one contracture release surgery before adulthood.

faqs.question: What is Z-plasty and how does it help contracture?
faqs.answer: Z-plasty is a surgical technique that repositions two triangular skin flaps to redirect the tension line of a scar band. A 60-degree Z-plasty lengthens the scar by 75%, allowing the joint to extend fully. It works best for linear neck, elbow, and finger contractures and has a recurrence rate below 15% when followed by rehabilitation.

faqs.question: What exercises help burn scar contracture?
faqs.answer: Sustained passive stretching (30-60 seconds per position) and active range-of-motion exercises performed three times daily are the foundation. Physiotherapists prescribe joint-specific protocols. Heat before stretching (warm shower or paraffin bath) and hydrotherapy improve compliance and outcomes. Exercises must continue for 12-24 months throughout scar maturation.

faqs.question: When should I see a surgeon for a burn contracture?
faqs.answer: See a plastic surgeon if joint range of motion remains restricted by more than 15 degrees after 3-6 months of consistent physiotherapy, splinting, and pressure therapy. Immediate surgical referral is needed for any contracture causing neurovascular compromise, airway risk from neck contracture, or joint subluxation.

faqs.question: Does silicone gel really help burn scars?
faqs.answer: Yes, silicone gel sheets and topical silicone have moderate-quality evidence showing a 30-40% reduction in scar thickness and redness compared with no treatment. They work by hydrating the stratum corneum and reducing TGF-beta activity. They are most effective when applied 23 hours per day from the time of wound closure for at least 6 months.

faqs.question: Can steroid injections treat burn contracture?
faqs.answer: Intralesional triamcinolone acetonide (10-40 mg/mL) injected into hypertrophic scar every 4-6 weeks flattens scar, reduces itch, and may slow early contracture band formation. Response rate is 70-80% for hypertrophic scars. Side-effects include local skin atrophy and depigmentation. It is most useful as an adjunct to stretching and compression.

faqs.question: Will a skin graft prevent contracture from coming back?
faqs.answer: Skin grafts reduce but do not eliminate secondary contracture. Split-thickness grafts have a 20-40% secondary contraction rate without postoperative compression and splinting. Full-thickness grafts have lower secondary contraction rates of 5-15%. Rehabilitation, pressure, and splinting after grafting are essential to maintain the range of motion gained surgically.

faqs.question: How is burn contracture different from a keloid?
faqs.answer: A keloid grows beyond the original wound boundary and rarely restricts joint movement. A contracture stays within the wound footprint but tightens the skin over a joint, limiting range of motion. Keloids are primarily cosmetic concerns; contractures cause functional disability. Both are treated by plastic surgeons but require different approaches.

metaTitle: Burn Scar Contracture: Symptoms & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Burn scar contracture: tightening of scar tissue after burns that restricts joint movement. Symptoms, causes, surgery options and rehabilitation reviewed here.
